ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Tandoori Chicken. Nearest parent is a spiced grilled chicken product. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Grilled meat dishes — spiced marinated whole chicken, tandoor-grilled (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for tandoori chicken; A03XS (grilled meat dishes) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for tandoori chicken as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tandoori chicken is approximately half a chicken (about 300g cooked weight, bone-in), yielding roughly 180–200g of edible meat.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; DB code F11020052, cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad; cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for tandoori chicken (DB code F11020052). Core macros and micronutrients cross-referenced with HPB Singapore food composition data for Indian dishes. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values derived from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for roasted spiced chicken.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tandoori Chicken — analogous estimate based on spiced marinated grilled chicken; GI 67 referenced from peer-reviewed GI measurement data for marinated grilled chicken preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); cross-referenced with published GI data for spiced marinated chicken dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on spiced marinated grilled chicken preparations with similar macronutrient profiles (low carbohydrate ~2.5g/100g, high protein ~24g/100g). The glycemic load per 300g serving is approximately 4.0, which is very low, consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content. The original input cited GI 67 from a recipe nutrition database; this value is retained as a reasonable analogous estimate pending direct measurement.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 382mg per 100g; approximately 1146mg per standard 300g half-chicken serving
hypertension.guidance: A 300g serving of tandoori chicken contributes approximately 1146mg sodium (~50% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit), primarily from marinade salt and tandoori masala; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g and request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5g/100g) and high protein (24.2g/100g) with a low glycemic load of 4.0 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori chicken is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.5g/100g), high lean protein (24.2g/100g), and a glycemic load of approximately 4.0 per 300g serving, resulting in negligible postprandial blood glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8g/100g), zero trans fat, and high lean protein; moderate sodium requires monitoring
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori chicken is a heart-friendly protein choice with low saturated fat (1.8g/100g), zero trans fat, and no added oils; however, the moderate sodium content (382mg/100g) warrants portion control for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, and the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) in the marinade may provide additional cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory marinade spices (turmeric, ginger, cumin) and high lean protein for muscle maintenance
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The tandoori marinade contains turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerol), and cumin (cuminaldehyde), which have documented anti-inflammatory properties; combined with high lean protein supporting muscle maintenance, tandoori chicken is a suitable protein source for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Chicken is one of the most iconic dishes of North Indian cuisine, originating in the Punjab region and now widely popular across India, Pakistan, and Indian diaspora communities worldwide, including Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish consists of chicken — typically a half bird or whole leg pieces — marinated for several hours in a vibrant paste of yoghurt (dahi), Kashmiri red chilli powder, turmeric, garam masala, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, and salt. The yoghurt tenderises the meat while the spices penetrate deeply. The marinated chicken is then cooked in a tandoor (traditional clay oven) at temperatures exceeding 400°C, producing a characteristic charred, smoky exterior with intensely flavoured, succulent meat inside. The vivid orange-red colour is derived from the combination of turmeric, red chilli, and sometimes food colouring. Tandoori chicken is served with mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein (24.2g/100g), low-fat (6.8g/100g), low-carbohydrate (2.5g/100g) dish with a caloric density of 163 kcal/100g, making it one of the healthiest preparations of chicken available. It is halal and gluten-free. The low glycemic load (~4.0 per 300g serving) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate at 382mg/100g, requiring portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The anti-inflammatory spices in the marinade (curcumin from turmeric, gingerol from ginger) add functional health value beyond basic nutrition.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (DB code F11020052), the primary approved source for Indian dishes, with cross-reference to HPB Singapore. Core macros and most micronutrients carry confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K carry confidence 0.9 from enriched JSON, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference ranges for roasted spiced chicken. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct University of Sydney GI Database entry exists for tandoori chicken.
notes: Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for tandoori chicken not available in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases as of 2026. Water content set to -1: moisture data for tandoori-cooked chicken not reported in the primary source. Trans fat confirmed 0.0 per ICMR-NIN source — naturally negligible in lean marinated grilled chicken. Copper (0.1mg/100g), manganese (0.05mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.5mcg/100g) sourced from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for roasted chicken with spices. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on spiced marinated grilled chicken preparations; no direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load of 4.0 per 300g serving is very low, consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content. Portions array from input contained several erroneous entries (pizza portion labels, duplicate 250g entries, chicken wings labels) that are not applicable to tandoori chicken; the canonical serving of 300g (half chicken, cooked, bone-in) from the verified source is used as the standard serving size. The FoodEx2 code A03XS is used as the closest defensible parent for grilled meat dishes; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for tandoori chicken.
